item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Best part of this is the subtle humor in the origin story.

The Foundation doesn’t even need to cover up the incident with the old man; as far as the public is concerned it’s just another chapter in the wacky adventures of Florida Man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Researcher Boyd ruined all the fun for the rest of us :(

+1, awesome SCP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Researcher Boyd: These things are too cute! So kitschy, and look how this one nuzzles my hand! Where'd we find them?

Researcher #2: Surrounding the eyeless corpse of an old man they had clearly pecked to death, Hitchcock style.

Boyd: Can I bring them treats on my lunch break?

R#2: ….. they don't eat. When was your last psych eval?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is entertaining. Flamingos are cool.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: New image up taken by me in my front yard

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Previously on the WikiWalk, SCP-243 was linked to SCP-1507, due to the possibility that 243's effects could have been the reason 1507 is animate. It seems like a good idea that 1507 should link back. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Adding the following Addendum:

Addendum 2: It is currently hypothesized that SCP-243, or a similar anomaly, may be a possible origin for SCP-1507. It is currently unknown why SCP-1507 has yet to deanimate, or if it will deanimate in accordance to SCP-243's behavior in the future. Investigation is ongoing.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: God, that Image...
item.content:encoded:
I love these flamingos, I love the fact that they are "alive" and personable.
I HATE that image. The beady eyes, the plastic beak, and the up close image really add a level of creep that gets to me
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, this has aged pretty well. The fact that the flamingos were allowed to interact with the staff (originally) is the only thing wrong with it. It's got good tone, a good concept (this one's subjective), and a good presentation. May these ridiculous yet good SCPs live on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Several tone mistakes, and even language mistakes, mar this one. The idea is very interesting and original, but I seriously doubt that the foundation would take something with an unknown level of intelligence and clearly capable to kill and label it "safe." It just doesn't make sense. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not everyone is going to agree with me on this but I like this? I have a thing where I think that some sites have a lower budgets then other sites (site-19 being the most rich because blah) and sometimes it is easier for some to breach containment.

EDIT : also I consider this a classic since it's Rogets first SCP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow this does not hold up at all. Plastic flamingos that attack people, who got angry because a staff member acted stupid. And they… can… successfully attack a Foundation site, despite having the durability of plastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If anything, they should want the free flamingos to come to them so they can contain them.

Well if the new flamingos aren't coming, that's probably good due to cutting down on potential storage issues and more incidents attacking guards. That said, yeah, I agree with the rest. Not a fan of this either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know this is an earlier work by a now very well-established contributor to the site, but I had to downvote (even accounting for the fact that this thing is a few years old at this point). It just hasn't aged well. Other SCPs have since done the whole "escalating hostility toward humanity" thing, and done it much better. The object itself, an aggressive and sapient flock of plastic lawn flamingos, is somehow both a boring Series I monster manual entry and also at the same time a smarmy, ironic, "weird for the sake of weird" bog-standard Series II entry. It's like it combines the worst of both worlds.

Finally, a plot note… they realized that there's more of these things outside of containment, and that the flamingos in containment are drawing them in. And the Foundation's solution to this is to soundproof the containment cell? And prevent other flamingos from coming? Why on Earth would they do that? The Foundation's job is to contain anomalies, not minimize flamingo-breaches. If anything, they should want the free flamingos to come to them so they can contain them. Re-writing the containment procedures with this in mind ("existing samples of SCP-1507 are to be used as lures to attract uncontained instances") would make the piece slightly more interesting. Probably still not interesting enough to secure my upvote, but it'd be something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Outdoor aviary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did 11 plastic flamingos infiltrate a foundation site and cause a containment breach?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Edited. That was awkward sounding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Instances of SCP-1507 are vulnerable to the same pressures ordinary plastic would be vulnerable to, such as heat and pressure.

The repetition of "pressure" sounds strange to me. Is there another word we can use instead?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I saw this I immediately though of a famous prank that happened in the '70s at the University of Wisconsin-Madison campus. On the first day of classes, 1008 plastic pink flamingos were discovered outside one building. One by one students grabbed them for souvenirs.

Making those 1008 examples of this scp would mess up your current timeline, but I personally see so much potential.

On a side note, the plastic pink flamingo is now Madison's city bird as of about '07.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't think why I didn't upvote this when it first went up— I think the polishing must have made the difference. It's so kitschy. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Herpaderp have a flamingo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yesh, we really polished this thing. You were great to work with and always had useful input. Good times!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I helped Roget with the tone and grammar on this thing as much as I could. I'm glad to see it's being seen positively.

The only content I suggested was the fact that it attacks the eyes. There was something about the thin metal leg-stands, which have been in the dirt a lot, being forced through someone's eye was something I think would work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Crows also remember individual humans who have annoyed them or displayed open hostility in some manner. Interesting article on crow memory

My husband can attest to this. His parents' house (where we lived for quite a long time) is in the middle of rural, woodsy country. It's also right under a common crow route to and from nesting areas, so there's always a lot of crows around. DH's charming habit of hauling his bb gun out to take shots at them taught the entire crow population of the area that the minute they see the back door open, they're gone. But only when HE steps out. When I or anyone else steps out, they just hang about making noise.

Highly social birds are not nearly as dumb as people want to think. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On a second read-through, I'm not seeing any. So, good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems silly, but we have killer grass, a lawn ornament that creates a plastic ecosystem, a pool that sterilizes itself, and many others.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a good point. I actually didn't know that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Makes me wonder if they can be taught to speak or at least understand human speech. Having hostile things around that hate you unconditionally is a bad idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess this is a weird thing to take issue with considering some of the other articles, but I keep coming back to the fact that these are killer lawn flamingos. It's not badly written, so I won't downvote, but I can't really upvote either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've heard of crows avoiding places where a flock member has gotten killed for years. I'd assume more intelligent entities would only hold a grudge longer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can see how some of those would improve the article.

Alright, done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I meant redactions in the more general sense, to include blackboxes, but those are gone now, so forget that part. As for tonal issues, these are all things that caught my eye personally. I use the imperative tense here a lot, so remember that it's just my opinion, which counts for whatever that's worth and no more.

Criticism
Hide

Staff members should not be allowed to access them without being accompanied by security. Any staff members entering the enclosure must wear protective clothing covering the head and body.

I would replace references to "them" with SCP-1507 and "the enclosure" with the particular name/number/room/what-have-you of the enclosure. For that matter, I would replace the Wilderness Observation Cell in the containment procedures with something more specific, something like "containment area 38 at Site 90210" or similar.

If SCP-1507 appears to be vocalizing more frequently than is considered normal, the on-site soundproofing equipment must be activated until the vocalizations cease.

I assume you mean noise-cancelling speakers or something (I'm not an expert in acoustics). You might mention that such equipment must be present in this particular cell/area for containment purposes before saying that that equipment should be turned on should the situation require it.

They are vulnerable

I generally don't like the word "they" as used in this article; it seems less formal than the manner in which a researcher would be writing.

SCP-1507 was first discovered on 09/18/1991, in Jacksonville,Fl. They first came to the Foundations attention when a foundation followed up on a 911 call where a man claimed to be harassed by his lawn flamingos.

Space between comma and state abbreviation, remove "they," don't use the word "first" twice in two consecutive sentences, use an apostrophe for possessive adjectives, and…actually, you might just rewrite the second sentence. "when a foundation followed" needs to be changed to whatever that phrase is supposed to say, "a 911 call where a man" is informal, and since "where a man claimed to be harassed by his lawn flamingos" isn't in quotation marks, it should use formal, clinical language as well (since it's a Foundation researcher summarizing an event in an official report).

Amended, see incident 1507-A and B

Incident 1507-A and -B. Capital and period.

The incident log could use a reformatting, maybe put both logs in individual quote boxes rather than just their descriptions, and I would bold the headers.

Finally, the part where their principal attack is to use their metal stakes as claws just doesn't work for me, and it's kinda the most important part, since it's how they're able to attack people at all. I try to imagine how they fling themselves forward, spin around so that their stakes are pointing towards someone, and then…recoil-shoot the pointy part into someone? Or maybe bounce around and stab people that way? I dunno, but it isn't working for me as presently written. And there are probably more fixable spots in the tone; I just pointed out the ones that struck me the most.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well one of the crossed out parts indicates they have a complex intelligence, so they're smarter than the average flamingo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed all the black boxes, can I get some tonal examples? There aren't any [REDACTED] in the article….did you read it carefully?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a pretty good idea. Several components of the writing (the number of redactions/blackboxes, typos, general clinical tone) just don't work for me, though. Downvote for now, may upvote later if edits are made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one is different from the gnomes, I believe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was under the impression we already had one of these… can anyone confirm this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tend to go "awww" at the first incident log, before the researcher kicking the puppy part. Are their attention spans similar to that of a real bird? If so, they should forget about the incident over time, provided that no new incidents occur.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So lawn decorations shaped like flamingos that call to their flock who proceed to attack humans? Pretty interesting. I wasn't quite aware of their risk to humanity at first, but I can see why they'd upgrade them to Euclid status.

Tonewise, there's not much that I can find that needs fixing. Perhaps removing a few of the blacked-out boxes would make your entry more readable, but otherwise it seems quite good. The idea unsettles me, in that attacking lawn flamingos are unsettling. That's all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: fix'd. Also, can you provide specific examples of tonal issues?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The story behind this is compelling (they were nice until someone messed with them), but it needs some work for tone. Also, U.S. state abbreviations are always two capital letters, FL in this case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Made it a bit longer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept, and the idea that they incorrectly filed these things. But I'd suggest adding a little to the first incident log. Having it end at 'SCP-1507 have become aggressive' seems a little anti-climactic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My second attempt at writing an article. At least I formatted it this time! With thanks to misterbibs.
